paper_title: Beyond Blacklists: Learning to Detect Malicious Web Sites from Suspicious URLs

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Understanding the scope and constraints of Part A paper selection
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Explain the key constraints for selecting a research paper in this exam — what venues are allowed, what methods qualify, and what makes a paper reproducible on a laptop?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Helped me organize the constraints into categories: venue (CORE A* only), year (2009-2012), method (SVM/GMM/ARIMA), and reproducibility (CPU-only, public data). I used this as a checklist while evaluating papers.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Cross-verified every constraint against the original PDF instructions provided by the instructor. All points matched.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Analyzing already-selected papers to avoid duplicates and identify gaps
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Analyze the existing submissions from the response sheet. What is the distribution across SVM, GMM, and ARIMA? Which types of papers are over-represented and where are there still gaps?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Revealed that SVM papers dominated with 55+ selections, GMM had about 15, and ARIMA only about 12. Most simple application-based SVM papers from ACL and KDD were already taken. This told me I needed to look for less obvious but still beginner-friendly application papers.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Opened the live Google Sheet response link and manually counted the method distribution. The numbers matched Claude's analysis.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Searching for a beginner-friendly SVM application paper not yet selected
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Find SVM application papers from KDD, ACL, EMNLP, or ICDM between 2009-2012 that apply SVM to a practical real-world task like classification or detection, use publicly available datasets, and have straightforward experimental setups reproducible with scikit-learn.
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude searched across multiple CORE A* venues and identified 'Beyond Blacklists: Learning to Detect Malicious Web Sites from Suspicious URLs' (Ma et al., KDD 2009) as a strong candidate. The paper applies SVM to detect malicious URLs — an intuitive binary classification task with a public dataset on UCI ML Repository.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Verified the paper exists on ACM Digital Library (DOI: 10.1145/1557019.1557153). Confirmed the UCI dataset is available at archive.ics.uci.edu/dataset/187/url+reputation. Read the paper abstract to confirm SVM is the primary method.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Understanding the core methodology of the selected paper
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Explain how SVM is used in this paper for URL classification. What types of features are extracted from URLs, and how does the classification pipeline work end to end?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Clarified that the paper extracts two types of features from URLs — lexical features (URL length, number of dots, presence of IP address, suspicious tokens) and host-based features (WHOIS age, geographic info, IP block). These features are fed into a linear SVM and SVM with RBF kernel for binary classification (malicious vs benign).
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Read Sections 3 and 4 of the paper to verify the feature extraction process and SVM setup. The explanation aligned with the paper's description of lexical and host-based feature sets.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Identifying limitations and failure cases of the paper's approach
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: What are the main limitations and failure cases of using SVM for malicious URL detection? When would this approach fail in practice?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Identified key limitations: the model cannot detect zero-day attacks with completely new URL patterns, adversaries can craft URLs to evade lexical features, host-based features depend on WHOIS data which can be spoofed, and the model needs retraining as attack patterns evolve over time.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Cross-checked with Section 6 of the paper where the authors discuss the adversarial nature of the problem and the need for online learning to adapt to evolving threats.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Validating the selected paper against all exam requirements and checking for duplicates
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Cross-check this paper against every requirement in the exam PDF — venue, year, method, contribution type, dataset, compute, and uniqueness. Is there any reason this paper would be rejected?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Systematically verified: KDD is in the allowed venue list, 2009 is within range, SVM is the primary method, it is an application/methodological paper, the dataset is public with no restrictions, and no GPU is needed. Also confirmed zero matches in the 93 existing submissions.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I independently checked: KDD is listed on page 2 of the PDF under allowed venues. The UCI dataset page shows CC BY 4.0 license. I searched the live response sheet for 'Beyond Blacklists' and 'Justin Ma' and found no matches.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Assessing reproducibility and planning the experimental setup for Part B
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: How can I reproduce the key experiments from this paper using scikit-learn? What subset of the dataset should I use, and what evaluation metrics should I report?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Suggested using scikit-learn's load_svmlight_file to load the UCI dataset, subsampling to 5K-10K URLs for laptop feasibility, training LinearSVC and SVC with RBF kernel, and reporting accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. The pipeline is about 30-50 lines of Python.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Checked scikit-learn documentation to confirm load_svmlight_file exists and supports the SVM-light format used by the UCI dataset. Also verified that LinearSVC and SVC classes are available in sklearn.svm module.

top_5_prompts.prompt: Find SVM application papers from KDD, ACL, EMNLP, or ICDM between 2009-2012 that apply SVM to a practical real-world task like classification or detection, use publicly available datasets, and have straightforward experimental setups reproducible with scikit-learn.
top_5_prompts.why_important: This was the most impactful prompt because it directly led to discovering my selected paper. By specifying the need for an application paper with public data and scikit-learn compatibility, it filtered out theoretical and heavy-compute papers, leading to a paper I can genuinely understand and reproduce.

top_5_prompts.prompt: What are the main limitations and failure cases of using SVM for malicious URL detection? When would this approach fail in practice?
top_5_prompts.why_important: Understanding failure cases early helped me assess whether I can reason about the paper critically in Part C. Knowing that adversarial evasion, zero-day attacks, and feature spoofing are key weaknesses gives me concrete points to discuss during the proctored exam.

top_5_prompts.prompt: Cross-check this paper against every requirement in the exam PDF — venue, year, method, contribution type, dataset, compute, and uniqueness. Is there any reason this paper would be rejected?
top_5_prompts.why_important: This validation step was critical to avoid disqualification. A single missed requirement — wrong venue, wrong year, duplicate selection — could cost me Parts B and C entirely. Systematic checking gave me confidence the selection is valid.

top_5_prompts.prompt: Analyze the existing submissions from the response sheet. What is the distribution across SVM, GMM, and ARIMA? Which types of papers are over-represented and where are there still gaps?
top_5_prompts.why_important: Understanding the landscape of already-taken papers was crucial. It showed me that most obvious sentiment analysis and text classification SVM papers were gone, pushing me to explore data mining and security applications where strong papers were still available.

top_5_prompts.prompt: How can I reproduce the key experiments from this paper using scikit-learn? What subset of the dataset should I use, and what evaluation metrics should I report?
top_5_prompts.why_important: Thinking ahead about reproducibility during paper selection itself helped me confirm that this paper is genuinely feasible for Part B. Knowing the exact tools, data loading method, and evaluation metrics in advance gives me a clear roadmap for reproduction.

student_declaration.statement: I declare that this JSON file contains a complete and honest record of my LLM usage for Part A.
